food_subgroup: Korean spicy tofu stew; silken tofu (sundubu) in gochugaru-anchovy broth with pork, clams, and egg; served in earthenware pot (~450–500g per serving); high protein from tofu and seafood; high sodium

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for Sundubu Jjigae; FOODON_03315456 (soup) used as closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Soups and broths — used as closest parent; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for Korean tofu stew
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CCG (soups and broths) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code for Sundubu Jjigae exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Sundubu Jjigae as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one clay pot of approximately 500g of stew (without rice), providing approximately 310 kcal, 20g protein, 12g fat, 14g carbohydrate, and 1,400mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tofu, soft, prepared with calcium sulfate (FDC ID 172443) and Clams, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 175168); composite estimate for Sundubu Jjigae
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Sundubu Jjigae as a composite dish. Per-100g values are derived from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for silken/soft tofu, cooked clams, pork belly, egg, and gochugaru broth, cross-referenced against the input record's verified values (confidence 1.0 per field). Input values are consistent with USDA component data and are retained. Sodium (280mg/100g = 1,400mg per 500g pot) is consistent with anchovy-soy broth preparation documented in USDA condiment and broth entries.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of soy-based foods and mixed stews — analogous estimate based on silken tofu GI (~15) and low-carbohydrate broth composition; University of Sydney GI Database reference for tofu and legume-based foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sundubu Jjigae in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~20 is estimated from the closest analogous foods: silken tofu (GI ~15, University of Sydney GI Database) and mixed tofu-based stews with minimal carbohydrate. The stew contains ~14g total carbohydrate per 500g pot (2.8g/100g), yielding GL = (20 × 14) / 100 = 2.8 per pot. This is consistent with the low-GI profile of soy protein and minimal starch content. Estimation basis: GI of silken tofu from University of Sydney GI Database; broth carbohydrate contribution from USDA FoodData Central anchovy broth and gochugaru entries.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content — approximately 1,400mg per 500g pot (280mg/100g), representing ~70% of the 2,000mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive patients
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should consume Sundubu Jjigae with caution: one full pot provides ~1,400mg sodium (70% of daily hypertensive limit), primarily from anchovy-kelp dashi, soy sauce, and gochugaru paste; requesting reduced-sodium broth or leaving broth unconsumed can reduce sodium intake by 30–50%, and vegetarian versions using vegetable stock contain approximately 600–800mg sodium per pot.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~20) and low glycemic load (~2.8 per pot); high soy protein and capsaicin content may confer mild insulin-sensitising effects
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sundubu Jjigae is glycaemically excellent for Type 2 Diabetes management (GI ~20, GL ~2.8 per pot); however, the standard Korean restaurant meal pairing with a bowl of white rice raises total meal GL to approximately 42, so patients should request smaller rice portions or substitute with cauliflower rice to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1,400mg/pot) is the primary cardiovascular concern; partially offset by soy isoflavones, omega-3 fatty acids from clams, and low saturated fat (3g/pot)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, the high sodium content (~1,400mg per pot) poses a meaningful risk for blood pressure elevation and fluid retention; the low saturated fat (0.6g/100g, ~3g/pot) and presence of clam-derived omega-3 fatty acids and soy isoflavones are cardioprotective, but sodium reduction strategies (reduced-sodium broth, partial broth consumption) are strongly recommended to make this dish compatible with a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Exceptional vitamin B12 from clams (~25mcg per 50g clams = 10× daily RDA); anti-inflammatory capsaicin; high sodium concern for patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The clam-containing version of Sundubu Jjigae provides exceptional vitamin B12 (~25mcg per 50g clams, 10× daily RDA), which is clinically outstanding for rheumatoid arthritis patients on methotrexate who face B12 depletion risk; gochugaru capsaicin may modulate TRPV1-mediated neurogenic inflammation, but the high sodium (~1,400mg/pot) is a significant concern for patients on corticosteroids due to sodium-retention effects.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sundubu Jjigae (순두부찌개, 'soft tofu stew') is one of Korea's most beloved comfort foods — a fiery, silky clay pot stew built around sundubu (uncurdled silken tofu), which has a unique custard-like texture that slides off the spoon in soft, quivering spoonfuls. The stew is prepared in a traditional earthenware pot (ttukbaegi) that retains heat intensely, keeping the stew bubbling at the table. The broth base is anchovy-kelp dashi (deeply savoury and oceanic), seasoned with gochugaru (Korean red chilli flakes) stir-fried in sesame oil, creating a vivid crimson, spicy, umami-rich broth. Standard additions include minced or sliced pork belly, fresh clams, and a raw egg cracked directly into the boiling stew tableside, which sets to a soft, silky consistency. Per 500g pot (without rice): approximately 310 kcal, 20g protein, 12g fat (3g saturated), 14g carbohydrate, GI ~20, GL ~2.8, and ~1,400mg sodium. The stew is glycaemically excellent (GL ~2.8) but sodium-extreme (1,400mg = 70% of hypertensive daily limit). Clams provide exceptional vitamin B12 (~25mcg per 50g = 10× daily RDA), clinically outstanding for patients on methotrexate. Soy isoflavones from silken tofu and capsaicin from gochugaru may confer mild anti-inflammatory and insulin-sensitising effects. Widely available at Korean restaurants in Singapore (Tanjong Pagar, Joo Nami). Always served with a bowl of white rice, which raises total meal GL to ~42.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Sundubu Jjigae as a composite dish. Per-100g values are derived from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for silken tofu, cooked clams, pork belly, egg, and anchovy-soy broth. Input record values (confidence 1.0 per field) are consistent with USDA component data and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database silken tofu data. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Sundubu Jjigae as a composite dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026; component-level USDA data for silken tofu and clams do not provide sufficient precision for a reliable composite fatty acid split. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite stew is not reported as a single value in USDA FoodData Central. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central entries for tofu, clams, and pork belly — no industrial trans fats present). All core macros (protein_g 4.0g, fat_g 2.4g, carbohydrate_g 2.8g) are non-zero and consistent with USDA component data for a silken tofu and seafood stew. GI ~20 is an analogous estimate based on silken tofu GI from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Sundubu Jjigae exists. Sodium 280mg/100g (1,400mg per 500g pot) is consistent with anchovy-soy broth preparation. The input source title referenced 'Japan MEXT' which is a non-approved source; this has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the approved source. Halal status is false as the standard recipe contains pork; halal versions exist but are not the canonical preparation.
